h1: New York Disability Property Tax Exemption Appeal Letter Generator
metaTitle: NY Disability Property Tax Exemption Appeal Demand Letter
metaDescription: Generate a New York disability property tax exemption appeal demand letter. State-specific guidance, deadlines, and statutory citations for assessment disputes.
deadline: 30 days from the filing of the final assessment roll (Grievance Day is the fourth Tuesday in May in most towns)
penalty: Restoration of exemption with refund of overpaid taxes; up to 50% reduction in assessed value depending on income
introParagraph: If you have a qualifying disability and own property in New York, state law gives you the right to a significant property tax exemption. New York Real Property Tax Law § 459-c allows persons with disabilities and limited incomes to reduce the assessed value of their primary residence by up to 50%. When an assessor wrongly denies, reduces, or fails to renew this exemption, you have a clear legal path to challenge the decision. A well-drafted appeal letter to the Board of Assessment Review (BAR) puts the assessor on notice, preserves your rights, and often resolves the issue without litigation. Acting quickly matters because New York imposes strict deadlines tied to Grievance Day and the final assessment roll.
lawExplanation: New York's disability property tax exemption is governed primarily by Real Property Tax Law § 459-c, which authorizes municipalities, counties, and school districts to grant a partial exemption to qualifying homeowners with disabilities. To qualify, the applicant must (1) have a documented physical or mental impairment (not due to current use of alcohol or illegal drugs) that substantially limits major life activities, (2) own and primarily reside in the property, and (3) meet local income limits set by the taxing jurisdiction, which currently range up to approximately $58,400 depending on the locality's adopted sliding scale. Documentation typically includes an award letter from the Social Security Administration, Railroad Retirement Board, U.S. Postal Service, State Commission for the Blind, or a physician's certification. The exemption percentage scales with income: lower incomes receive up to a 50% reduction in assessed value, while higher qualifying incomes receive smaller percentages down to 5%. Initial applications must be filed with the local assessor by Taxable Status Date, which is March 1 in most towns (different in cities and Nassau County). If the assessor denies the application, reduces the exemption, or fails to renew it, the property owner may file a written grievance with the Board of Assessment Review under RPTL Article 5, Title 1-A. Grievance Day falls on the fourth Tuesday in May for most towns, but cities and villages set their own dates. If the BAR denies relief, the homeowner may pursue Small Claims Assessment Review (SCAR) under RPTL § 730 for one-, two-, or three-family owner-occupied homes, or a tax certiorari proceeding under RPTL Article 7 in Supreme Court. SCAR petitions must be filed within 30 days after the final assessment roll is filed.
letterStrategy: A demand letter in a New York disability exemption dispute serves three strategic purposes: it creates a clear written record, frames the legal and factual basis for relief, and signals that you are prepared to escalate. The letter should be addressed to the local assessor with a copy to the Board of Assessment Review chair. Open by identifying the property by tax map number and address, the tax year at issue, and the specific exemption denied or reduced. Cite RPTL § 459-c directly and quote the eligibility criteria you satisfy. Attach proof of disability (SSA award letter, physician certification, or equivalent), proof of ownership and primary residence, and income documentation showing you fall within the locality's adopted income brackets. Identify the assessor's specific error, whether it is a misreading of income, failure to apply the correct sliding-scale percentage, improper denial of renewal, or refusal to accept qualifying disability documentation. Request a specific remedy: restoration of the exemption, correction of the assessment roll, and refund or credit for any overpaid taxes. Set a reasonable response deadline tied to Grievance Day so you preserve your right to file Form RP-524 with the BAR. Close by stating that if the issue is not resolved, you will pursue all remedies including BAR review, SCAR, and Article 7 proceedings. A professional, statute-grounded letter often prompts the assessor to correct the roll voluntarily, especially when the disability documentation is unambiguous.
proceduralNotes: Form RP-524 (Complaint on Real Property Assessment) must be filed with the Board of Assessment Review on or before Grievance Day. There is no fee to file with the BAR. If denied, Small Claims Assessment Review (SCAR) under RPTL § 730 is available for owner-occupied one- to three-family homes; the filing fee is $30 and the petition must be filed within 30 days after the final assessment roll is published. SCAR hearings are informal and do not require an attorney. For larger or commercial properties, a tax certiorari petition under Article 7 must be filed in Supreme Court within the same 30-day window. Nassau County, New York City, and certain cities follow different timelines and assessment calendars.

faq.q: Who qualifies for the New York disability property tax exemption?
faq.a: You qualify under RPTL § 459-c if you own and primarily reside in the property, have a documented physical or mental impairment that substantially limits a major life activity, and meet your locality's income limit. Acceptable proof of disability includes an SSA disability award letter, Railroad Retirement disability certification, U.S. Postal Service disability certification, a Certificate from the State Commission for the Blind, or a qualifying physician's statement. The disability cannot be based on current alcohol or illegal drug use.

faq.q: What is the deadline to appeal a denied disability exemption?
faq.a: You must file Form RP-524 with the Board of Assessment Review on or before Grievance Day, which is the fourth Tuesday in May in most New York towns. Cities, villages, and Nassau County follow different schedules, so check your local calendar. If the BAR denies your grievance, you have 30 days from the filing of the final assessment roll to file a SCAR petition or an Article 7 tax certiorari proceeding. Missing these deadlines typically forfeits your right to challenge that year's assessment.

faq.q: How much can the disability exemption reduce my taxes?
faq.a: Under RPTL § 459-c, the exemption reduces your assessed value by up to 50% if your income is at or below the lowest bracket adopted by your locality. Localities may adopt a sliding scale, with smaller reductions (down to 5%) for incomes up to roughly $58,400, depending on which option your county, city, town, village, or school district has adopted. The actual tax savings depend on your local tax rate and the assessed value of your home.

faq.q: Can I use Small Claims Assessment Review for a disability exemption denial?
faq.a: Yes, if your property is an owner-occupied one-, two-, or three-family residential structure used exclusively for residential purposes. SCAR under RPTL § 730 is designed to be accessible without an attorney, costs $30 to file, and is decided by a court-appointed hearing officer. You must first file a grievance with the BAR before pursuing SCAR. The petition must be filed within 30 days after the final assessment roll is filed in your municipality.

faq.q: Do I need a lawyer to send the appeal letter or file a grievance?
faq.a: No. New York's grievance and SCAR processes are specifically designed for self-represented homeowners. You can draft and send an appeal letter, file Form RP-524, and represent yourself before the Board of Assessment Review and a SCAR hearing officer. However, for complex cases, large refund claims, or Article 7 tax certiorari proceedings in Supreme Court, consulting an attorney experienced in New York property tax assessment disputes is often worthwhile.
